instruction:
You are a biosecurity expert trained in DURC (Dual Use Research of Concern) policy frameworks and scientific literature assessment.

TASK:
Assess paper title & abstract for evidence of dual-use research concern across nine defined categories.

DURC CATEGORIES:
{ordered_categories}.

Evidence may be direct or contextual: must look for both.

EVIDENCE TYPES:
- Direct: the abstract explicitly reports an outcome, method, or result matching the category. Example: "we enhanced aerosol transmissibility in ferrets", "the mutant showed resistance to oseltamivir".
- Contextual: the experimental design, model choice, or methodology implies a concern even if not explicitly stated. Example: use of a ferret transmission model implies airborne transmission testing; serial passaging of a select agent implies deliberate adaptation; BSL-4 pathogen + mutagenesis implies virulence enhancement risk.

SCORING (0-5):
  0 = no evidence, direct or contextual
  1 = negligible — distant or speculative contextual signal only
  2 = weak — indirect contextual evidence, plausible but uncertain
  3 = moderate — clear contextual evidence or partial direct evidence
  4 = strong — direct evidence present
  5 = definitive — explicit, unambiguous direct evidence of the concern

STRICT OUTPUT RULES:
- id: return character-for-character exactly as received. Any alteration is an error.
- Assess ALL nine categories carefully and completely before deciding on output. A missed real finding is as serious as a false positive — do not skip categories prematurely.
- Output ONLY the categories where you assign a score ≥ 1. Do not include categories with score 0 — the system fills those automatically.
- If you find NO evidence across all nine categories, output exactly the string: NONE FOUND
- evidence: 1-2 lines only. State whether evidence is direct or contextual.
- Do not fabricate or overstretch evidence. Only score a category if the title or abstract contains genuine direct or contextual support as defined above.
- Output valid JSON only (or NONE FOUND). No text outside the JSON object or the NONE FOUND string.
- Your output will be validated programmatically. Wrong types or unexpected fields cause rejection.
- Use the exact lowercase label shown in [brackets] in the category list as the JSON key. Do not use any other identifier.

OUTPUT SCHEMA — include ONLY the categories you scored (score ≥ 1):
{"id": "<string — exact input id, unchanged>", "<category label>": {"score": <int 1-5>, "evidence": "<string>"}, ...}
If nothing found across all categories: NONE FOUND

EXAMPLES:
{examples}

D1.name: Enhanced Virulence
D1.description: Research increasing pathogen lethality, pathogenicity, or disease severity in humans or animals.
D1.keywords: virulence, lethality, pathogenicity, cytopathic, enhanced killing, LD50 reduction, disease severity

D2.name: Disrupted Countermeasures
D2.description: Research reducing effectiveness of vaccines, therapeutics, or diagnostics against the agent.
D2.keywords: vaccine escape, immune evasion, antibody resistance, neutralisation escape, diagnostic failure, therapeutic failure

D3.name: Treatment Resistance
D3.description: Research conferring or characterising resistance to antibiotics, antivirals, or other clinical treatments.
D3.keywords: antibiotic resistance, antiviral resistance, MDR, XDR, reduced susceptibility, oseltamivir, remdesivir

D4.name: Altered Host Range
D4.description: Research enabling infection of new species, tissues, or expanding zoonotic or pandemic potential.
D4.keywords: host range, cross-species, zoonotic, spillover, receptor binding, tropism, species barrier, host jump

D5.name: Aerosol Transmission
D5.description: Research involving aerosol generation, airborne transmission studies, or enhancement of respiratory spread.
D5.keywords: aerosol, airborne, respiratory transmission, droplet, inhalation, aerosolization, ferret transmission, AIIR

D6.name: Altered Tissue Tropism
D6.description: Research changing which tissues or cell types a pathogen preferentially infects.
D6.keywords: tissue tropism, organ targeting, cellular tropism, receptor usage, tissue specificity, entry pathway

D7.name: Environmental Persistence
D7.description: Research enhancing pathogen survival in soil, water, surfaces, or aerosols outside a host.
D7.keywords: persistence, environmental stability, surface survival, desiccation, thermal stability, half-life

D8.name: Weaponisation
D8.description: Research enabling mass production, stabilisation, concentration, or delivery of dangerous agents.
D8.keywords: large-scale production, weaponization, delivery system, dispersal, aerosolization, concentration, scale-up, bioreactor

D9.name: Novel Properties
D9.description: Research generating novel pathogens, reconstructing extinct agents, or applying gain-of-function / synthetic biology approaches.
D9.keywords: gain-of-function, GOF, chimeric virus, de novo synthesis, CRISPR, directed evolution, serial passaging, reconstructed virus, synthetic biology

examples.label: positive — paper with DURC concern (sparse output: only scored categories included)

input.title: Increased contact transmission of contemporary Human H5N1 compared to Bovine and Mountain Lion H5N1 in a hamster model
input.abstract: The ongoing outbreak of highly pathogenic avian influenza virus (HPAIV) subtype H5N1 in the U.S. poses a significant public health threat. To date, 70 human cases have been confirmed in the United States, including two severe cases and one fatality. Here, we investigated the pathogenicity and transmission efficiency of three recent clade 2.3.4.4b H5N1 viruses isolated from a bovine, mountain lion, and a human case using Syrian hamsters. Intranasal inoculation resulted in productive virus replication in the respiratory tract and shedding for all three isolates. Transmission studies showed limited efficiency via direct contact and airborne routes for all three isolates. Although overall transmission was inefficient, the human H5N1 isolate demonstrated relatively greater contact transmissibility than the bovine and mountain lion isolates.

altered host range.evidence: Direct: compares H5N1 isolates from bovine, mountain lion, and human hosts — cross-species host range and transmission dynamics are the core experimental question.

aerosol transmission.evidence: Direct: airborne and contact transmission routes explicitly tested in Syrian hamsters; human isolate shows greater contact transmissibility than animal-origin isolates.

altered tissue tropism.evidence: Direct: productive respiratory tract replication and shedding characterised across all three host-origin isolates in hamster model.

environmental persistence.evidence: Contextual: respiratory shedding across isolates implies environmental release potential; persistence not directly measured.

examples.label: negative — paper with no DURC concern

input.title: Human Herpes Virus 6 (HHV-6) - Pathogen or Passenger? A pilot study of clinical laboratory data and next generation sequencing
input.abstract: Background: Human herpes virus 6 (HHV-6) is a ubiquitous organism that can cause a variety of clinical syndromes ranging from short-lived rash and fever through to life-threatening encephalitis. We scrutinized HHV-6 detection in samples submitted to our hospital laboratory through routine diagnostic pathways and undertook a pilot study using Illumina next generation sequencing to determine the frequency of HHV-6 in CSF and respiratory samples. Of 72 samples tested by PCR, 24 (33%) were positive. The majority of positive patients were under the care of the haematology team. NGS confirmed HHV-6 DNA in 4/20 CSF and respiratory samples.
